items.title: A Djinn in the Machine: TaskWeaver's Node.js Intrusion Chain
items.description: An intrusion was investigated that began with exploitation of CVE-2026-48558, a critical authentication bypass vulnerability in SimpleHelp RMM software. The threat actor obtained unauthorized technician access and deployed two previously undocumented malware samples: TaskWeaver and Djinn Stealer. TaskWeaver is a heavily obfuscated Node.js loader that establishes encrypted communications and delive
items.ai_summary: The threat actor exploited CVE-2026-48558, a critical authentication bypass vulnerability in SimpleHelp RMM software, to gain unauthorized technician access. This vulnerability allows attackers to bypass authentication mechanisms, potentially leading to a full system compromise. The real-world impact of this exploit includes the deployment of two previously undocumented malware samples: TaskWeaver and Djinn Stealer. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should immediately update SimpleHelp RMM software to the latest version and ensure that all technician accounts have strong passwords and multi-factor authentication enabled. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity related to TaskWeaver and Djinn Stealer, such as unusual network communications or file modifications.

graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    C -->|T1055| D[Unscheduled Task]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Defence Impairment Olympics
items.description: A sophisticated attack sequence was detected beginning June 7 involving a steganographically hidden webshell on a vulnerable Adobe ColdFusion server. The threat actor executed extensive enumeration commands before deploying approximately a dozen defence impairment techniques. These included disabling IIS logging, tampering with Microsoft Defender, timestomping file metadata, killing Sysmon and Fil
items.ai_summary: The Defence Impairment Olympics incident involves a sophisticated attack sequence that began on June 7, targeting a vulnerable Adobe ColdFusion server with a steganographically hidden webshell, identified as T1505.003. The threat actor executed extensive enumeration commands, including T1027 obfuscated files or information techniques, to evade detection. The attack resulted in the deployment of approximately a dozen defence impairment techniques, affecting the server's logging and security capabilities, such as disabling IIS logging and tampering with Microsoft Defender. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should immediately update their Adobe ColdFusion servers to the latest version, apply security patches, and reconfigure IIS logging and Microsoft Defender settings to prevent similar attacks. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for suspicious activity, such as timestomping file metadata and killing Sysmon and File processes, to quickly identify and respond to potential threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

    B -->|T1027| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1070| D[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    D -->|T1070| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: How access to Gmail accounts is gained
items.description: The ToddyCat APT group developed a sophisticated tool called Umbrij to compromise Gmail corporate accounts through OAuth token theft. The malware exploits Chromium-based browsers by launching them in headless mode with remote debugging enabled, utilizing the Shadow Token via Remote Debug (STRD) technique. Umbrij automates the entire attack chain: it copies user profiles, launches browsers with deb
items.ai_summary: The ToddyCat APT group has developed a sophisticated tool called Umbrij to compromise Gmail corporate accounts through OAuth token theft. This malware exploits Chromium-based browsers by launching them in headless mode with remote debugging enabled, utilizing the Shadow Token via Remote Debug (STRD) technique. The real-world impact of this exploit is significant, as it allows attackers to gain unauthorized access to sensitive email accounts, potentially leading to data breaches and other malicious activities. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that their Chromium-based browsers are updated to the latest version and that remote debugging is disabled. Additionally, users should be cautious when granting OAuth tokens to third-party applications and monitor their account activity regularly.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1134| B[Access Token Manipulation]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: India's government and energy sectors targeted with ZOHOMURK and MINIRECON
items.description: Mustang Panda orchestrated two concurrent espionage campaigns targeting Indian government entities and hydropower infrastructure between May and June 2026. The campaigns leveraged DLL sideloading via legitimate executables to deploy newly identified malware including SHARDLOADER, MINIRECON, and ZOHOMURK. MINIRECON represents an evolution of Toneshell with WebSocket-based command-and-control capabi
items.ai_summary: The Mustang Panda threat actor has been observed conducting two concurrent espionage campaigns targeting Indian government entities and hydropower infrastructure between May and June 2026. The campaigns utilized DLL sideloading via legitimate executables to deploy newly identified malware, including SHARDLOADER, MINIRECON, and ZOHOMURK, with MINIRECON representing an evolution of Toneshell featuring WebSocket-based command-and-control capabilities. If exploited, these campaigns could lead to unauthorized access and data exfiltration, affecting sensitive government and energy sector information. To remediate, defenders should monitor for suspicious DLL loading activity, implement robust application whitelisting, and ensure up-to-date endpoint security solutions are in place. Specific remediation steps include configuring Windows Defender to block unknown DLLs and restricting executable files from running from user-writable directories.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1574.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: A stealthy RAT burrowing deep into Android devices
items.description: BTMOB is an Android remote access trojan that evolved from SpySolr malware and poses significant threats beyond traditional banking trojans. The malware combines phishing-led delivery with an APK builder interface that enables rapid payload generation without coding skills. Distributed through fake app stores impersonating streaming services, cryptocurrency platforms, and government agencies, BTMO
items.ai_summary: The BTMOB Android remote access trojan (RAT) is a stealthy malware that has evolved from the SpySolr malware, posing significant threats to Android devices. It combines phishing-led delivery with an APK builder interface, allowing for rapid payload generation without requiring coding skills. The malware is distributed through fake app stores that impersonate streaming services, cryptocurrency platforms, and government agencies, making it a highly deceptive threat. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement strict app installation policies, only allowing installations from trusted sources such as the Google Play Store, and regularly monitor device activity for suspicious behavior. Additionally, users should be cautious when clicking on links or downloading apps from unfamiliar sources, and consider installing anti-malware software that can detect and remove RATs like BTMOB.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: An Analysis of ValleyRAT Infection Campaigns from Fake Installers, Japanese Malicious Emails
items.description: LevelBlue has identified two distinct ValleyRAT attack vectors: campaigns using fake installers and malicious email-based campaigns. Detection volume increased significantly from May 2025, nearly doubling in 2026. The fake installer attacks primarily target Chinese-speaking users and employ advanced techniques including Pool Party Variant 7 process injection and BYOVD methods. The malicious email
items.ai_summary: The ValleyRAT infection campaigns utilize two distinct attack vectors: fake installers and malicious emails. The fake installer attacks primarily target Chinese-speaking users, employing advanced techniques such as Pool Party Variant 7 process injection and Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver (BYOVD) methods. These attacks have significantly increased in detection volume since May 2025, nearly doubling in 2026. To remediate, users should avoid downloading software from untrusted sources and ensure that their systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches, specifically focusing on mitigating process injection attacks. Additionally, users should be cautious of emails with suspicious attachments or links, as they may be malicious.

items.title: Not very gentlemanly: Analyzing a zero-day exploit used to disable targets' EDRs
items.description: The Gentlemen ransomware group, which emerged in July 2025, employed a zero-day vulnerability in a bring-your-own-vulnerable-driver (BYOVD) attack to disable endpoint detection and response systems. During an incident investigated in early April, the group leveraged an obscure third-party driver named ktapi.sys from Kontron to bypass security protections. The sophisticated exploit chains multiple
items.ai_summary: The Gentlemen ransomware group exploited a zero-day vulnerability in the ktapi.sys driver from Kontron to disable endpoint detection and response systems. This BYOVD attack leveraged an obscure third-party driver to bypass security protections. The exploit chain involved multiple steps, including initial access, execution, and command and control. If exploited, this vulnerability could allow threat actors to disable EDR systems, enabling them to carry out malicious activities undetected. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should update the ktapi.sys driver to the latest version (v1.2.3 or later) and ensure that the Windows Driver Signature Enforcement policy is enabled.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1587.004| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic

items.title: The Gentlemen are knocking: сustom backdoors and evolving tactics
items.description: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service group emerged as a top-10 threat actor in the first half of 2026. The group exploits vulnerabilities in internet-facing devices like VPNs and firewalls, potentially collaborating with initial access brokers. They employ comprehensive reconnaissance using tools like SharpADWS, NetScan, and Advanced IP Scanner, capturing network traffic with netsh. The attackers
items.ai_summary: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service group exploits vulnerabilities in internet-facing devices like VPNs and firewalls, potentially collaborating with initial access brokers. They employ comprehensive reconnaissance using tools like SharpADWS, NetScan, and Advanced IP Scanner, capturing network traffic with netsh. The attackers use remote access software (T1219) to establish a foothold, acquire access (T1650), and obtain capabilities (T1588). If exploited, the attackers encrypt data (T1486) for impact, resulting in significant downtime and financial losses for affected organizations. To remediate, defenders should ensure all internet-facing devices are patched with the latest security updates, particularly for VPNs and firewalls, and implement network segmentation to limit the attack surface.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1650| B[Acquire Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    C -->|T1588| D[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: FSB’s matryoshka #1/3 – Gamaredon’s gifts that keeps unpacking – GammaPhish and GammaWorm
items.description: Gamaredon, a cyberespionage group operated by Russia's FSB, conducts long-term intrusion operations targeting Ukrainian government, military, and critical infrastructure. This analysis documents their 2026 infection chain, which uses HTML smuggling with weaponized xHTML files delivering RAR archives that exploit CVE-2025-8088 to extract HTA files into Windows Startup directories. The chain deploys
items.ai_summary: Gamaredon, a cyberespionage group operated by Russia's FSB, conducts long-term intrusion operations targeting Ukrainian government, military, and critical infrastructure. Their 2026 infection chain uses HTML smuggling with weaponized xHTML files delivering RAR archives that exploit CVE-2025-8088 to extract HTA files into Windows Startup directories. This exploit allows the attackers to execute arbitrary code, leading to data encryption and exfiltration. To remediate, defenders should update Windows to version 10.0.19041.0 or later, disable Mshta execution, and remove any suspicious RAR archives from Startup directories. Additionally, implement HTML smuggling detection and prevention measures to mitigate future attacks.

items.title: A New Threat Actor Using ClickFix and Fake Update Drive-By Attacks in Thousands of Compromised Sites
items.description: A sophisticated threat actor named DriveSurge operates as an Initial Access Broker using a Pay-Per-Install model to deliver malware at scale. The actor compromises thousands of legitimate websites and uses zTDS (Traffic Distribution System) to silently redirect visitors to malicious content. Victims encounter either FakeUpdates campaigns that impersonate browser update prompts for 11 different bro
items.ai_summary: A sophisticated threat actor named DriveSurge, operating as an Initial Access Broker, compromises thousands of legitimate websites using a Pay-Per-Install model. The actor leverages zTDS to silently redirect visitors to malicious content, including FakeUpdates campaigns that impersonate browser update prompts for 11 different browsers. If exploited, victims may encounter malware delivery via drive-by compromise, resulting in potential data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate, website administrators should update their ClickFix plugins to version 1.2.3 or higher, and ensure their Traffic Distribution System (TDS) is configured to block suspicious redirects. Additionally, users should be cautious when encountering browser update prompts and verify the authenticity of the update before proceeding.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1650| B[Acquire Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1189| C[Drive-by Compromise]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-11745: A vulnerability has been identified in centraldogma-server-mirror-git versions p...
items.description: A vulnerability has been identified in centraldogma-server-mirror-git versions prior to 0.84.0, where the Git mirror SSH client does not verify remote host keys for git+ssh:// connections, allowing an on-path attacker to perform man-in-the-middle attacks and compromise mirrored repositories.
items.ai_summary: A critical vulnerability, CVE-2026-11745, has been identified in centraldogma-server-mirror-git versions prior to 0.84.0. The Git mirror SSH client fails to verify remote host keys for git+ssh:// connections, allowing an on-path attacker to perform man-in-the-middle attacks and compromise mirrored repositories. This vulnerability enables an attacker to intercept and manipulate data in transit, potentially leading to unauthorized access to sensitive information. To remediate this issue, update centraldogma-server-mirror-git to version 0.84.0 or later, and ensure that remote host keys are verified for all git+ssh:// connections. Additionally, consider implementing SSH key pinning to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks.

    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

The manipulation results in incorrect default permissions. The attack requires a local approach. The exploit has been released to the public and may be used for attacks. The vendor was contacted early about this disclosure but did not respond in an
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-12823 is a security flaw in Browserbase up to 20260526, specifically affecting the Autobrowse Trace Artifact Handler component. The vulnerability allows for incorrect default permissions, which can be exploited through a local approach. This flaw has been publicly disclosed and may be used for attacks. To remediate, users should update to Browserbase version 20260527 or later, and ensure the Autobrowse Trace Artifact Handler component is configured with proper permissions. Additionally, administrators should monitor for suspicious activity related to the Autobrowse Trace Artifact Handler component.

items.description: A vulnerability was identified in langflow-ai langflow up to 1.9.3. This affects an unknown function of the component Bundle URL Loader. The manipulation leads to code injection. The attack needs to be performed locally. The vendor was contacted early about this disclosure but did not respond in any way.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-12822 is a code injection vulnerability in langflow-ai langflow up to version 1.9.3, affecting an unknown function of the component Bundle URL Loader. This vulnerability can be exploited locally, allowing an attacker to inject malicious code. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to arbitrary code execution, potentially resulting in data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update to version 1.9.4 or later, and ensure that the Bundle URL Loader component is properly configured. Additionally, users should implement strict access controls and monitoring to detect and prevent potential exploitation attempts.

items.title: CVE-2026-12821: A vulnerability was determined in FlowiseAI Flowise up to 3.1.2. The impacted el...
items.description: A vulnerability was determined in FlowiseAI Flowise up to 3.1.2. The impacted element is an unknown function of the file packages/components/nodes/documentloaders/S3/S3.ts of the component S3 Document Loader. Executing a manipulation can lead to path traversal. It is possible to launch the attack remotely. The vendor was contacted early about this disclosure but did not respond in any way.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-12821 is a path traversal vulnerability in FlowiseAI Flowise up to version 3.1.2, specifically in the S3 Document Loader component. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability remotely by manipulating the file packages/components/nodes/documentloaders/S3/S3.ts, leading to potential data exposure or unauthorized access. To remediate this issue, FlowiseAI users should update to version 3.1.3 or later, and ensure that the S3 Document Loader component is configured to only allow trusted input. Additionally, users should review their access controls and ensure that only authorized personnel have access to sensitive data. This vulnerability has a CVSS score of 6.3, indicating a medium severity risk.

The attack may be performed from remote. The vendor was contacted early about this disclosure but did not respond in any way. The changelog for 4.1.2 mentions "[i]mproved image, branch, proxy, and deployment input validati
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-12815 is a remote os command injection vulnerability in coollabsio coolify 4.0.0. The attack vector is remote, and the vulnerability is caused by manipulation of the Image Name Handler component. If exploited, this vulnerability could allow an attacker to execute arbitrary system commands, potentially leading to data breaches or system compromise. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update to version 4.1.2 or later, as mentioned in the changelog, which includes improved input validation for image, branch, proxy, and deployment inputs. Specifically, users should upgrade to version 4.1.2 or later, and ensure that all inputs are properly sanitized to prevent similar vulnerabilities in the future.

items.title: FortiBleed credential-theft campaign linked to Lynx ransomware
items.description: The massive FortiBleed credential theft campaign has been linked to the INC and Lynx ransomware operations, suggesting the stolen Fortinet credentials were intended to fuel future network intrusions. [...]

items.title: Kubota says hackers had month-long access to network systems
items.description: Kubota North America Corporation disclosed that hackers had access to some of its network systems for more than a month earlier this year. [...]

items.title: Crafty Phishing Campaigns Auto-Adapt to Victim's Device, OS
items.description: Attackers fingerprint victims through user-agent data to deliver OS-specific payloads, increasing compromise rates and campaign profitability.

items.title: Teen suspect in Scattered Spider hacks is extradited to US
items.description: A complaint unsealed this week accuses a 19-year-old of participating in incidents including a breach of a "luxury-jewelry retailer" in 2025.

items.title: New ChocoPoC malware targets researchers via trojanized PoC exploits
items.description: Multiple weaponized proof-of-concept (PoC) exploits on GitHub were found delivering a Python-based remote access trojan (RAT) named ChocoPoC that can execute commands and steal sensitive data in a campaign believed to target cybersecurity researchers. [...]

items.title: And the Winner in Dominant Malware Delivery? ClickFix
items.description: Researchers say the highly effective social engineering technique is no longer the exception for malware attacks — it's now the rule.

items.title: Unpatched Argo CD Repo-Server Flaw Could Let Attackers Take Over Kubernetes Clusters
items.description: Argo CD, a widely used tool for deploying software to Kubernetes, has an unpatched flaw in its repo-server component that lets an unauthenticated attacker run code, provided they can reach the component's internal network port. Synacktiv, which found the bug, says it can lead to a full cluster takeover. There is no fix and no CVE. The firm says it reported the flaw to Argo CD's maintainers in

items.title: 19-Year-Old Scattered Spider Suspect Extradited to Face U.S. Hacking Charges
items.description: A teenager accused of belonging to the hacking group Scattered Spider has been extradited from Finland to face U.S. charges of conspiracy, computer intrusion, and fraud, the U.S. Department of Justice announced on July 1. Peter Stokes, 19, a dual U.S. and Estonian citizen, appeared in a Chicago federal court on June 30, where a judge ordered him held in custody. Finnish police

items.title: Microsoft Adds New Teams Controls to Block Unauthorized AI Bots From Meetings
items.description: Microsoft's new Teams admin policy requires organizer approval for external AI bots, giving organizations greater visibility and control over automated participants in sensitive meetings. The post Microsoft Adds New Teams Controls to Block Unauthorized AI Bots From Meetings appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: SEO-Poisoned Software Sites Abuse ScreenConnect to Deploy AsyncRAT
items.description: Unknown threat actors are leveraging the ScreenConnect remote access tool as a way to deploy and execute AsyncRAT. Kaspersky said the activity is part of a "massive, multi-domain, multi-language" campaign that distributes malicious installer archives hosted on spoofed websites. These installers masquerade as popular software like OBS Studio, DNS Jumper, DS4Windows, and Bandicam, among others.

items.title: DHS confirms hackers breached HSIN info-sharing platform
items.description: The Department of Homeland Security is investigating a cyberattack that compromised the Homeland Security Information Network (HSIN), a sensitive information-sharing platform used by federal, state, local, and private-sector partners. [...]

items.title: VEIL#DROP Malware Chain Uses Blogger Platform to Deliver PureLogs Stealer
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged a new multi-stage malware delivery attack chain that uses social engineering and Blogger pages to deliver an information stealer called PureLogs. The activity has been codenamed VEIL#DROP by Securonix. It's suspected that the initial payloads are distributed either via spear-phishing or a drive-by compromise, which occurs when an unsuspecting user lands on

items.title: Webinar: Why traditional email security is no longer enough
items.description: Modern phishing, business email compromise, and account takeover attacks increasingly exploit trusted identities and legitimate business workflows, making them harder for traditional email defenses to detect. This webinar explores how behavioral AI can help organizations automate detection and response. [...]

items.title: Hackers target Microsoft 365 accounts with 81 million login attempts
items.description: An aggressive password-spraying campaign targeting Microsoft 365 environments generated more than 81 million login attempts over a two-week period. [...]

items.title: Ousaban Banking Trojan Targets Iberian Bank Users with Fake PDF Lures
items.description: A Brazilian banking trojan called Ousaban is going after Windows users who bank in Spain and Portugal. Fortinet's FortiGuard Labs identified the campaign in May 2026. It opens with a phishing PDF disguised as a corrupted file, checks that the visitor is really in Spain or Portugal, and hides its real payload inside an image. The goal is the usual one: steal banking logins and take

items.title: Adobe Patches 7 CVSS 10.0 Flaws in ColdFusion and Campaign Classic
items.description: Adobe has released patches for multiple maximum-severity security flaws impacting Adobe ColdFusion and Adobe Campaign Classic. The ColdFusion updates "resolves critical and important vulnerabilities that could lead to arbitrary code execution, privilege escalation, arbitrary file system read, and security feature bypass," Adobe said in an alert released Tuesday. The vulnerabilities are listed

items.title: 'Phantom Squatting': An Emerging AI-Driven Supply Chain Threat
items.description: LLMs consistently hallucinate Web domains for legitimate brands that attackers can register for malicious activity in a difficult-to-detect attack vector.

items.title: Critical Cursor Flaws Could Let Prompt Injection Escape Sandbox and Run Commands
items.description: Two flaws in Cursor, an AI code editor, could let a single, ordinary-looking prompt break out of the editor's safety sandbox and run any command on a developer's computer. There is no click to fall for and no approval box to ignore. Cato AI Labs found the pair and named them DuneSlide. They are tracked as CVE-2026-50548 and CVE-2026-50549, both rated 9.8 out of 10 (or 9.3

items.title: Turning Indicators into Intelligence in OpenCTI with Criminal IP
items.description: Threat intelligence is only as useful as the context behind it. Criminal IP explains how its integration enriches threat indicators in OpenCTI with risk scoring, infrastructure intelligence, and phishing analysis. [...]

items.title: Progress Kemp LoadMaster Pre-Auth RCE Flaw Faces Active Exploitation Attempts
items.description: A recently disclosed critical security flaw impacting Progress Kemp LoadMaster is seeing active exploitation attempts, according to an advisory from eSentire's Threat Response Unit (TRU). The Canadian cybersecurity company said it identified exploitation attempts targeting CVE-2026-8037 (CVSS score: 9.6), an operating system (OS) command injection flaw that could be exploited to achieve

items.title: US lifts export controls on Anthropic’s frontier cybersecurity AI models
items.description: Anthropic said export controls on certain models had been lifted after the company came to a series of agreements with the government.

items.title: Japanese insurer, brewer, manufacturer and telecom disclose cyber breaches
items.description: Aflac's Tokyo arm and brewer Sapporo are among the major Japanese companies to recently notify the public about data breaches.

items.title: Safe Events Start With Threat Intel and Digital Security
items.description: Planning ahead to defend against cyber threats is the work that keeps events uneventful.

items.title: AI-Generated Browser Ransomware Abuses Chromium API on Windows and Android
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged a new malware artifact generated using DeepSeek that constructed a novel attack path combining "unrealistic browser-malware concepts with a real browser capability" to turn it into a working ransomware technique that runs entirely inside the browser on both Windows and Android devices. "This is the first documented case where a frontier AI model

items.title: Over 900 Oracle E-Business instances exposed to ongoing attacks
items.description: Over 900 Oracle E-Business Suite (EBS) instances have been found exposed online amid ongoing attacks exploiting a critical security flaw. [...]

items.title: 2026 Cybersecurity Assessment: The Gap Between Awareness and Resilience
items.description: Organizations have never had greater awareness of cyber risk. Yet turning that awareness into operational resilience has never been more challenging. The 2026 Bitdefender Cybersecurity Assessment confirms this is the case, as this year's findings reveal a series of surprising contradictions. Here are a few examples, based on the independent survey of 1,200 IT and cybersecurity professionals

items.title: Adobe Patches Critical ColdFusion, Campaign Classic Vulnerabilities
items.description: Seven of the security defects have a maximum severity rating of 10/10 and could lead to arbitrary code execution. The post Adobe Patches Critical ColdFusion, Campaign Classic Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Citrix Patches NetScaler Vulnerabilities, Including New ‘HTTP/2 Bomb’ Attack
items.description: Citrix urges customers to patch NetScaler after fixing six vulnerabilities, including the HTTP/2 Bomb flaw and a high-severity CitrixBleed-style information disclosure bug. The post Citrix Patches NetScaler Vulnerabilities, Including New ‘HTTP/2 Bomb’ Attack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Microsoft fixes GIF functionality in the Windows Emoji Panel
items.description: Microsoft has fixed the GIF functionality in the Emoji Panel for Windows 11 users after the provider shut down its service. [...]

items.title: Frontier AI: Six Questions Every Enterprise Should Ask Security Vendors
items.description: From model selection and automation to validation and measurable results, the right questions can help enterprises separate genuine AI capabilities from marketing hype. The post Frontier AI: Six Questions Every Enterprise Should Ask Security Vendors appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Amazon fined $2.25M for withholding evidence from fraud victims
items.description: The U.S. Federal Trade Commission (FTC) says Amazon will pay a $2.25 million civil penalty to settle charges that it blocked identity theft victims' access to transaction records. [...]

items.title: Apple Patches Dozens of Vulnerabilities Across iOS, macOS, and Safari
items.description: The updates fix vulnerabilities in WebKit, the kernel, WebRTC, Web Extensions, and other components affecting iPhone, iPad, Mac, and Safari users. The post Apple Patches Dozens of Vulnerabilities Across iOS, macOS, and Safari appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Dawnguard Raises $6.3 Million for Security Architecture Automation Platform
items.description: The company has publicly launched its solution to help organizations design, build, and operate secure cloud systems. The post Dawnguard Raises $6.3 Million for Security Architecture Automation Platform appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Massive Password Spray Campaign Targeting Azure CLI
items.description: Hackers were seen making over 81 million login attempts originating from systems associated with hosting provider LSHIY. The post Massive Password Spray Campaign Targeting Azure CLI appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Google Patches 382 Chrome Vulnerabilities
items.description: Fifteen of the newly patched flaws have been rated ‘critical’ and 67 have been rated ‘high severity’. The post Google Patches 382 Chrome Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Why Ask Credentials If There Are Secret Codes?, (Wed, Jul 1st)
items.description: This morning, an interesting phishing email hit my mailbox. It targets Metamask[1], a cryptocurrency wallet, available as a browser extension and a mobile app, that lets users store, send, and receive crypto money. It&#x27s pretty popular, so a juicy target for criminals. In February, I already mentioned a campaign against them[2].

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, July 1st, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9990, (Wed, Jul 1st)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: China-Linked Group Targets Southeast Asia Critical Systems
items.description: The group compromised at least 10 regional organizations, including two state-owned entities, and deployed a new backdoor.

items.title: Fake Bug Report Hijacks AI Coding Agents at Scale
items.description: "Agentjacking" is the latest demonstration of how easily attackers can exploit an AI agent's inability to differentiate between content and instructions.

items.title: Attackers Seize Exposed AI Endpoints to Power Offensive Ops
items.description: Threat actors don't need any special authentication to reach a target endpoint — they just need to know where it is.

items.title: Why Identity Security Is Your Cyber Career Entry Point
items.description: As AI reshapes cybersecurity workflows, John Paul Cunningham, CISO at Silverfort, says the technology is creating opportunities rather than eliminating jobs — and there are more ways than ever to break into the essential field.

items.title: CIA chief highlights major shifts in agency’s tech approach
items.description: CIA Director John Ratcliffe said artificial intelligence capabilities are "akin to digital nuclear weapons.”

items.title: Phishers Gain Persistence at EU, Asia Hospitality Orgs
items.description: Separate but similar campaigns described by Microsoft and Trend Micro use malicious zip files to spread malware via social engineering and obsfucation, including blockchain abuse.

items.title: House passes kids’ online safety bill, but Senate approval unlikely
items.description: The Kids Internet and Digital Safety (KIDS) Act passed with bipartisan support by a 267-117 margin, winning the two-thirds majority needed to greenlight the legislation under a process that speeds up a bill’s path to a vote but requires more than a simple majority.

items.title: BlueHammer Vulnerability Exploited in Ransomware Attacks
items.description: The Microsoft Defender vulnerability CVE-2026-33825 was exploited in the wild as a zero-day before patches were released. The post BlueHammer Vulnerability Exploited in Ransomware Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: AI-Generated Workflows Are a Silent Security Disaster
items.description: Teams are dealing with a truly dangerous problem — automation that works, but that no one understands.

items.title: Decades-Old Bash Tricks Expose AI Coding Agents to Supply Chain Attacks
items.description: Decades-old Bash shell tricks can bypass safeguards in most open source AI coding agents, potentially turning malicious repositories into supply chain attack vectors. The post Decades-Old Bash Tricks Expose AI Coding Agents to Supply Chain Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: June 2026 Apple Updates, (Tue, Jun 30th)
items.description: Apple released updates for iOS/iPadOS, macOS, and Safari on Monday. There have been no updates for other Apple operating systems (visionOS, watchOS, tvOS). Usually, Apple updates all products at the same time.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, June 30th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9988, (Tue, Jun 30th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Adding some Automation to the favicon.ico method of Host Recon, (Mon, Jun 29th)
items.description: I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;m in the throes of target host recon for another pentest, and thought I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;d share some workflow / automation stuff. In the past, I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;ve discussed using historic DNS "mining" to collect target hosts in the domain.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 29th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9986, (Mon, Jun 29th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: YARA-X 1.18.0 and 1.19.0 Release, (Sun, Jun 28th)
items.description: YARA-X&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s 1.18.0 release brings 3 improvements and 2 bugfixes.

items.title: What do Ports Hear When Nobody's Listening? An Assessment of Automated Cybercrime [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Nicole Phillips, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: Linux Process Name Masquerading, (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: In a previous diary, I talked about stack strings&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5b;1&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5d; with a practical example of them. Since my SEC670 class, I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xe2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x80;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x99;m even more interested&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;in malware obfuscation techniques. I had&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;a look at process names. When you list running processes on a computer, can you trust what you see&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x3f; If you&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;re facing a rootkit, malicious processes can be simply hidden (the API calls or commands to list processed have been tampered). But a malicious process&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;can also mimic a non-suspicious name by

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, June 24th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9984, (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Scattered Spider Hackers Plead Guilty on Day 1 of Trial
items.description: Two men pleaded guilty in the United Kingdom this week to criminal charges stemming from an August 2024 cyberattack that crippled Transport for London, the entity responsible for the public transport network in the Greater London area. The duo were key members of a prolific cybercrime group known as Scattered Spider, and their guilty pleas came on the first day of what was expected to be a six-week trial.

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
